questions.prompt: In the lesson’s definition, what does it mean to “overcome” fear in Scripture?
choices: Learning to trust and stand in the Lord in the middle of fear
Eliminating every fearful feeling so it never returns
Proving toughness by acting like nothing affects you
Avoiding situations that make you nervous at all costs

questions.prompt: Psalm 27:1 answers fear primarily by pointing you to:
choices: How strong your willpower is when pressure hits
Who the Lord is to you—your light, salvation, and strength
How safe your circumstances become over time
How other people should treat you to keep you calm

questions.prompt: If fear is “driving the bus,” what is the lesson’s implied alternative driver?
choices: Your emotions, as long as they feel sincere
Your past experience, because it has the most data
The authority of God’s Word shaping your responses
Random chance—nothing is really in control anyway

questions.prompt: Why does Psalm 27:1 start with God’s identity statements before the questions (“Whom shall I fear?”)?
choices: Because naming God’s character reframes the threat and grounds confidence
Because questions are always sinful and should be avoided
Because danger is usually imaginary if you think positively
Because David was trying to impress others with religious speech

questions.prompt: In the lesson, “The LORD is my light” most closely means the Lord:
choices: Gives you good luck when you make the right choices
Exposes and guides—showing what’s true when confusion presses in
Makes you feel spiritual by giving you a sudden emotional rush
Guarantees you will never face hard news again

questions.prompt: In the lesson’s explanation, “my salvation” in Psalm 27:1 emphasizes:
choices: Rescue and deliverance rooted in the Lord, not in your own cleverness
A religious label that proves you’re better than others
A promise that life will be comfortable from now on
A one-time ritual that automatically fixes your habits

questions.prompt: Calling the Lord “the strength of my life” is explained as God being:
choices: A distant helper who steps in only after you do your part
A stronghold/fortress—the One holding your life together
A motivator who boosts confidence but doesn’t change outcomes
A principle of positivity you can activate with the right mindset

questions.prompt: Which statement best reflects the lesson’s logic: “If the Lord is my salvation…”
choices: Then I can ignore consequences and do what I want
Then no enemy gets to decide my destiny apart from God’s permission
Then I won’t need other believers in my life
Then I should never admit fear out loud

questions.prompt: According to the lesson, fear is not finally answered by:
choices: Clarifying who God is and trusting Him personally
Pumping yourself up with self-confidence and toughness
Turning your eyes higher than the danger to God over it
Making David’s confession your own by faith

questions.prompt: The lesson says a man can quote verses and still be ruled by fear if he never learns to say:
choices: “I will do better this time, no matter what”
“My circumstances will surely improve soon”
“He is mine, and I am His”
“People should respect me so I can calm down”

questions.prompt: From the psychological section, fear and anxiety are described first as:
choices: Proof you are weak or “crazy”
A normal alarm system God designed for survival that can get stuck on
A sign you should isolate until you feel better
Evidence you don’t really love God

questions.prompt: Fight, flight, or freeze is mentioned to show that anxiety often involves:
choices: A body response that treats imagined or remembered danger like present danger
A spiritual gift that only certain Christians have
A problem solved mainly by pretending nothing happened
A guarantee that you will make the best decision under pressure

questions.prompt: In reentry, “hypervigilance” is described as:
choices: Getting bored because life feels too quiet
Constantly scanning for threats, waiting for disrespect or bad news
Refusing to ever think about the future
Being naturally fearless because you’ve “seen it all”

questions.prompt: The lesson describes anxious “what if” thinking as:
choices: A helpful tool that always prepares you for the best outcome
The mind running worst-case scenarios on repeat while the body reacts as if it’s happening
A sin because questions are never allowed
A sign that your faith is fake in every case

questions.prompt: Which is an example of fear “wearing a mask,” as described in the lesson?
choices: Quiet worry showing up as anger or intimidation on the surface
Confidence showing up as generosity and patience
Peace showing up as steadiness under pressure
Joy showing up as gratitude in hardship

questions.prompt: Past trauma is said to sometimes “wire” the brain to:
choices: Expect danger in relationships and react with anxiety when someone gets close
Become immune to fear forever
Forget painful memories completely once you join a program
Trust everyone automatically as a sign of healing

questions.prompt: The lesson says people handle anxiety better when they have:
choices: Safe relationships, predictable routines, and healthy calming practices
More social media input and constant news updates
No rules so they can express themselves freely
Pressure to act tough so nobody sees weakness

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson say psychological tools have limits?
choices: Because breathing and routines never help at all
Because they can’t answer the deepest question of control and ultimate safety
Because Christians should reject all insights about the body and mind
Because fear is always cured instantly if you try hard enough

questions.prompt: In the integration section, psychology mainly describes:
choices: How anxiety affects body and mind
How to earn righteousness through discipline
How to interpret prophecies about the future
How to avoid all emotions as unspiritual

questions.prompt: In the integration section, theology adds which deeper test to anxious thoughts?
choices: “Does this thought line up with who God is and what He has promised?”
“Does this thought make me feel strong and in control?”
“Will this thought keep people from correcting me?”
“Can I prove this thought is always true by my past?”

questions.prompt: The lesson contrasts “reasonable thinking” with a deeper shift toward:
choices: Being more suspicious so you aren’t surprised
Man-centered thinking to God-centered thinking
Ignoring the body and focusing only on feelings
Letting fear decide quickly so you don’t hesitate

questions.prompt: When fear rises in the house, the lesson recommends first learning to:
choices: Blame someone else so you feel justified
Pause, recognize what’s happening, and turn upward in prayer
Make a snap decision before anyone can challenge you
Withdraw completely so nobody can see weakness

questions.prompt: Which practice best matches the week’s application: “fear inventory”?
choices: Listing fears honestly, then pairing each one with Psalm 27:1 as a faith response
Listing fears to prove you have more problems than others
Listing fears so you can avoid anything uncomfortable this week
Listing fears and then trying to forget them immediately

questions.prompt: Why does the lesson say the goal is not to “magically erase” fear?
choices: Because fear is always good and should be obeyed
Because the goal is connecting real anxieties to the real God who stands over them
Because Scripture has nothing to say about emotions
Because fear cannot change in any way

questions.prompt: If fear tends to come out as anger when you feel disrespected, the lesson’s suggested response is to:
choices: Pause and pray before responding, choosing a different response because the Lord is your strength
Prove you’re not weak by escalating the conflict
Ignore the situation and pretend you don’t care
Keep the anger inside until it blows up later

questions.prompt: The lesson says anxiety often shrinks when it is brought into:
choices: A private space where nobody can challenge you
The light of God’s Word and the support of God’s people
A loud argument so you can win the point
A constant distraction so you never have to think

questions.prompt: How does the lesson frame overcoming fear as part of discipleship?
choices: It’s mainly a self-improvement project to earn respect
It trains you to steady others later, turning your story into a strengthening testimony
It proves you are naturally braver than other men
It removes your need for community because you become independent

questions.prompt: In the narrative assignment, personalizing Psalm 27:1 is meant to help you:
choices: Treat the verse as poetry without practical use
Make God’s truth personal—moving from general ideas to lived confession
Show off your Bible knowledge to others
Replace prayer with journaling

questions.prompt: “Breathing Psalm 27:1” is described as:
choices: A mystical chant to manipulate spiritual energy
A way to tie your breath to God’s Word so your body learns calm in response to Scripture
A substitute for trusting God in real situations
A technique that only works if you never feel fear at all

questions.prompt: Which summary best matches the closing encouragement of the lesson?
choices: Fear disqualifies you from walking with God until you fix it
God is not surprised by your fear; He invites you to bring it to Him and learn where to run
The best way to beat fear is to become harder and more detached
If you struggle with fear, you should stay silent to avoid shame
